# PRODUCTION.md

## Publishing Guide

Huddle already gives you the iOS client. To make it production-ready, keep the app thin, move privileged work behind infrastructure you control, and validate the real backend before you ship.

Generated by 10x on 2026-05-17T12:48:12Z.

## Production Foundation

Start by being explicit about what the shipped app should do and what should move behind infrastructure.

### What You Already Have

Huddle currently has 15 generated source files. Treat this project as the product surface and app shell, not the trust boundary.

- The app can own UI, local state, rendering, caching, and requests to services you control.
- `tenx/` contains local 10x metadata and preview support files. It is not production infrastructure.
- `PRODUCTION.md` is the handoff guide for replacing preview assumptions with a real backend.

### What Cannot Live In The App

Only values that are safe to expose should ship in the binary. Anything that can spend money, access private data, or enforce business rules should stay off-device.

- No secret-like environment variables are configured right now, which is a good time to decide what must stay off-device before launch.
- No public config keys are configured right now.
- If a value can spend money, access private data, or call a provider with elevated privileges, move it behind your backend.

```env
PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL=https://api.yourapp.com
SUPABASE_URL=https://your-project.supabase.co
SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=ship-only-if-rules-are-correct
OPENAI_API_KEY=server-only
```

## Recommended Backend Stack

Most teams only need a small amount of backend code. Supabase Edge Functions are part of the supported production path, so the decision is where auth, secrets, and durable data should live.

### Choose Vercel When

Vercel is the simplest choice when you mainly need server functions, webhook handling, scheduled jobs, or a thin API layer in front of vendors.

- You want fast deployment for API routes, AI proxy endpoints, Stripe webhooks, or cron-style jobs.
- You already have a Next.js site or expect to add one soon.
- Your database needs are light or already handled elsewhere.

### Choose Supabase When

Supabase is the better default when auth, relational data, storage, permissions, and Edge Functions are core product requirements.

- You need user accounts, Postgres tables, file uploads, admin views, realtime data, or named backend functions.
- You want auth, database, storage, SQL migrations, and server-side logic in one place.
- You are comfortable treating publishable client keys as public and enforcing access with Row Level Security and function auth.

### Use 10x Backend When

The managed Backend workspace is the right path when you want 10x to scaffold and operate named Supabase Edge Functions for secret-backed API work.

- `supabase/functions/<name>/index.ts` is the source of truth for each named endpoint.
- Backend-only secrets belong in Backend and sync to Supabase. Do not ship them as client runtime variables.
- If Backend deploys are blocked, treat that as a Supabase project plan or capability issue, not a reason to move secrets back into the app.

### Use Both When

Supabase plus Vercel is still useful when you also need a web app or server surfaces outside Supabase, but it is no longer the only production path.

- Use Supabase for auth, Postgres, storage, user-owned data, and any Edge Functions that sit close to that data model.
- Use Vercel for a Next.js site, vendor webhooks that already live there, or jobs that should stay outside Supabase.
- Keep the app talking to the smallest API surface that supports the real user flows.

## Production Hardening

The production version should survive bad networks, bad inputs, and real user data.

### Security And Auth

Enforce permissions in infrastructure you control instead of trusting the client to do the right thing.

- Do not trust client conditionals for auth, billing, feature gating, or write permissions.
- Audit every network request and move privileged provider calls behind your backend.
- Remove preview-only shortcuts, seeded demo accounts, and debug-only toggles from release builds.

### Reliability And Observability

Launch gets easier once errors are visible and recoverable.

- Add request timeouts, retry rules where appropriate, and user-facing recovery states.
- Log API failures, provider latency, background job failures, and critical business events server-side.
- Track usage and cost-sensitive actions where they actually happen instead of relying on client guesses.

## Release Path

Use TestFlight as the last systems check, then finish the App Store-specific details.

### Before TestFlight

Invite testers only after the production stack is real enough to learn from.

- Run the app against the real production-style backend instead of preview-only assumptions.
- If you use Backend, deploy the named Supabase Edge Functions you depend on and smoke-test them before inviting testers.
- Confirm backend secrets are synced remotely before TestFlight instead of relying on local-only configuration.
- Test sign-in, purchases, uploads, AI calls, and failure states on physical devices and TestFlight.
- Confirm analytics, logs, and crash reporting are visible before inviting external testers.

### Before The App Store

Finish the release paperwork while the build is still fresh in your head.

- Prepare screenshots, privacy answers, support contact info, review notes, and legal URLs.
- Write down the simplest explanation of your backend architecture for future maintenance and onboarding.
- Freeze the launch checklist and only submit after the critical items are actually complete.
